comments.text: Hooley in this paper has proved under the assumption of the extended Riemann Hypothesis, that integers $n$ such that for all $1<2^k<n$ the number $n-2^k$ is prime < $O(x^{1-\lambda+\epsilon})$ for any sufficiently small positive number $\epsilon$, where $\lambda = \Pi_p (1- \frac{1}{p(p-1)})$. This was further improved by Narkiewicz in this paper to $O(x^{1-\lambda/\log2 +\epsilon})$ under the same assumption. P.S. Improving on the result of Mientka and Weitzenkamp, Uchiyama and Yorinaga have proved there are no other such $n\leq 2^{77}$ in a two paper series - [1] and [2].

Let $E_2(N)$ denote the number of natural numbers $n \leq N$ for which $n - 2^k$ is a prime number for all $k$ with $1 \leq k \leq \log_2 n$. Vaughan, in this paper, has showed that for some suitable positive number $c$ and for every sufficiently large $N$,\[E_2(N) < N \exp (- \frac{c \log N \log \log \log N}{\log \log N})\]Furthermore, Mientka and Weitzenkamp in this paper has verified that $7, 15, 21, 45, 75,$ and $105$ are the only values of $n$ for which $n - 2^k$ is a prime number for all $k$ with $1 \leq k \leq \log_2 n$ for $n \leq 2^{44}$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Standard probabilistic heuristics suggest that the answer to this question is true, but only barely, and proving this rigorously seems well out of reach of current technology.

Why it suggests it barely? If $2^k<n<2^{k+1}$, then except for $n-2^k$, all values to check are of the form $n-2^i>2^{k-1}$. Hence the number of primes of the form $n-2^i$ is on average bounded by $3.$ Of course, there is some positive correlation on the chance, due to e.g. even vs odd, but so wondering which more detailed heuristical thoughts you had.

For each $n$ in this range, there are $k$ values to check, each of which has about a $1/k$ chance of being prime, so the chance that they are all prime is about $1/k^k$... which dominates the total number of $n$ in this range, which is $2^k$ or so, but not by a huge margin... $\exp(-k \log k)$ versus $\exp(k)$.

Ok, the terms barely and huge margin are here maybe subjective. This reasoning could apply to the "Erdos could not even prove..." part as well. Since $\exp(k) \exp(-k \log k) = \exp(- \Theta(k \log k))$, the chance of having really large counterexamples is very small. But starting from $n>10^4$, it is indeed still a chance, and a formal proof for excluding that is far from obvious. One deduces similar formulas for the probability (e.g. using Poisson approximation) that it is above $c \log k$, and since we are speaking about the limit, that should be the case.

I guess for me "barely" is with regards to how optimal the bounds need to be in order to conclude the result. For instance if the heuristic prediction for density was $\exp(-k \log k)$ and one managed somehow to get an argument that gave an upper bound of the shape $\exp(-k/\log k)$, this would usually be considered extremely close to the optimal bound, but in this case would be insufficient to conclude (even heuristically) that there are only finitely many counterexamples.
